title: The Cost of Togetherness: Why the Gulf States Must Renegotiate Defense Pacts
content:
The Gulf states must renegotiate their defense pacts with the United States in light of recent conflicts. The region has already begun to move in this direction, and the urgency has only increased. These negotiations are a minimum condition of sovereign security in a volatile area. The choices of a powerful patron now carry existential consequences for its regional partners. The Gulf states must insist on a seat at whatever negotiating table emerges from the current crisis.

For three decades, the Gulf states have been among the most consequential partners of American military power anywhere in the world. They have provided land, airspace, logistics, and political backing for operations across the Middle East. They have done so without formal treaties of mutual defense or the institutional protections afforded to European allies. What they received in return was an implicit promise that the American presence on their soil would protect them. However, recent wars have proven that this presence actually made them targets.

The Gulf states did not choose to participate in this wider war, yet they are expected to absorb its tremendous costs. A structural revision of the existing defense agreements is needed to address this dangerous asymmetry. The old arrangement, where host governments lack a voice in strategic decisions, cannot hold. Gulf states must be given a political framework that allows them to shape the terms of their own security and the broader regional settlement.
